title: Stunting vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Stunting](#dod:stunting) measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Stunting vs. GDP per capita
Stunting measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of children under five who are stunted and GDP per capita across countries in 2024, with GDP values expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices and plotted on a logarithmic axis. The vertical axis measures the percentage of children who are stunted, ranging from 0% to over 50%, while the horizontal axis spans from under $2,000 to over $100,000. Points are colored by region, distinguishing North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The data shows a clear negative relationship: countries with lower GDP per capita generally have higher stunting rates, while those with higher GDP per capita have lower rates. At the upper end of the stunting scale, Burundi, Niger, and the Democratic Republic of Congo sit above 45% stunting with GDP per capita below $2,000. Angola and Guatemala also appear in the high-stunting band, between 40% and 50%, with GDP per capita around $5,000 to $10,000. In the middle of the distribution, countries like India, Pakistan, and Cambodia cluster between 20% and 35% stunting with GDP per capita in the $5,000 to $10,000 range. Toward the lower right, nations such as Brunei, Estonia, and Bulgaria show stunting rates below 10% with GDP per capita exceeding $20,000. The lowest stunting rates, under 5%, are visible in countries with GDP per capita above $50,000, including several in Europe and North America. The pattern shows that higher GDP per capita coincides with lower shares of stunted children, with the steepest declines in stunting occurring as GDP per capita rises from under $5,000 to around $20,000.
